Impact of Anaerobic Selector Volume on Internal Carbon Storage and Post-Anoxic Denitrification Kinetics

Yizhou Pan¹, Lin Sun¹, Ahmad Shabir Razavi¹, Kyle Murray², Ahmed Al-Omari³, Mark Miller³, Martha Dagnew¹
¹ Dept. of Civil & Environmental Engineering, Western University, London, ON  ·  ² Greenway Wastewater Treatment Centre, London, ON  ·  ³ Brown and Caldwell, Phoenix, AZ
WEAO Technical Symposium
& OPCEA Exhibition
April 12–14, 2026
Niagara Falls, ON
1 Background

Challenge: Meeting tight effluent nitrogen limits forces many WWTPs to dose expensive external carbon (methanol) — costing money, adding operational risk, and creating supply-chain dependencies.

Our approach: Eliminate external carbon by maximising the carbon already present in raw influent — stored inside the bacteria as polyhydroxyalkanoates (PHA).

Key organisms: Polyphosphate-accumulating organisms (PAOs) take up volatile fatty acids (VFAs) during the anaerobic phase, using glycogen & poly-P as energy to synthesise and store PHA.

Post-anoxic denitrification — placing the anoxic zone after full nitrification — forces bacteria to use their stored PHA as the sole carbon source for NOₓ-N → N₂ conversion. No raw carbon remains at this stage.

Key question: If PAOs have more time in the anaerobic zone, do they store more PHA — and does that translate to faster, deeper nitrogen removal?

Research objective: Quantify how anaerobic selector contact time (1.0 h vs. 0.5 h) affects:

  • Internal PHA (PHB) accumulation
  • Specific Denitrification Rate (SDNR)
  • Long-term effluent NOₓ-N quality
2 Process Flow — Post-Anoxic Denitrification
① Anaerobic
⚡ The Charging Station
  • VFA uptake from raw wastewater
  • PHA synthesis & storage
  • Glycogen & Poly-P consumed
  • Phosphorus released to solution
② Aerobic
🔧 Preparation Phase
  • Nitrification: NH₄⁺-N → NOₓ-N
  • Glycogen recovery begins
  • Luxury P uptake
  • Bacteria recharge for anoxic
③ Post-Anoxic
🎯 The Harvest Phase
  • No external carbon available
  • Stored PHA oxidised
  • Denitrification: NOₓ-N → N₂
  • Effluent quality determined here
If the anaerobic "battery" is not fully charged, the post-anoxic stage fails — effluent NOₓ rises. Anaerobic contact time is the critical design variable.
3 Methods — Bench-Scale Setup

Fig. 1. Schematic of parallel SBR configuration. Both reactors fed from the same influent tank (Greenway WWTP, London ON). Only the anaerobic selector duration differs between SBR-1 and SBR-2.

PhaseSBR-1SBR-2
Anaerobic selector1.0 h0.5 h
Aerobic 2.0 h2.0 h
Post-Anoxic 2.5 h2.5 h
Settle + Decant 0.5 h1.0 h
Total Cycle6.0 h6.0 h
14h
HRT
20d
SRT
Fed from a single shared tank using raw influent from Greenway WWTP. Only variable: anaerobic selector duration.
📷 Reactor Installation at Greenway WWTP

Fig. 2. Bench-scale SBRs installed on-site at Greenway Wastewater Treatment Centre, London ON.

Both SBRs operated in parallel under identical conditions — same influent, same HRT/SRT, same aeration strategy. Only the anaerobic selector duration differed.
